behaviors.description: The cron parser rejects invalid expressions (e.g., month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) with a descriptive error message.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is parser validation/error construction in one local component (MOCK-MISS=no cross-module requirement). No real OS/network/subprocess (REAL-INFRA=no), no model output dependency (LLM-DEP=no), no multi-step agent pipeline (MULTI-STEP=no). Rejected Integration because behavior text is strictly about parser correctness and message content, not module handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Call the parser function with invalid cron strings (`13` month, `32` day, malformed tokens) and assert it returns/throws structured errors containing specific field-level reasons.

behaviors.description: The next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron expressions, including edge cases like "last day of month" and "nearest weekday."
behaviors.justification: Primary risk is calendar arithmetic logic inside run-time computation; this can fail without any external boundary. No required real infra or subprocess; deterministic inputs/outputs (REAL-INFRA=no, LLM-DEP=no). Rejected Integration because behavior text targets calculator correctness itself, not parser+calculator wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke next-run calculator directly with prebuilt schedule objects and fixed start times; assert exact next-N timestamps for normal schedules plus `L` (last day) and nearest-weekday edge cases across multiple months.

behaviors.description: The overlap detector identifies when two schedules would fire within the configured minimum gap (e.g., 5 minutes) and reports the conflicting time windows.
behaviors.justification: Core failure is interval comparison/window reporting logic in overlap detection; can be reproduced with local timestamp sequences. No real OS/network/subprocess needed (REAL-INFRA=no). Rejected Integration because behavior text does not require proving cross-module persistence/transport, only overlap computation and reported windows.
behaviors.test_description: Feed detector two deterministic run-time lists plus `min_gap=5m`; assert it flags overlaps within threshold and returns exact conflict windows (start/end pairs) including boundary conditions at exactly 5 minutes.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across a DST transition — a job scheduled for 2:30 AM handles the spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity correctly.
behaviors.justification: Real failure typically appears at module handoff between cron schedule computation and timezone/DST resolution rules (MOCK-MISS=yes for boundary bugs). Needs multiple local modules wired with real timezone-rule data usage, but not real network/subprocess (REAL-INFRA does not force System). Rejected System because behavior text only requires DST-correct scheduling logic, not OS-level clock/event/subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run scheduler end-to-end in-process (parser + next-run calculator + timezone resolver) for a real DST-observing zone; verify spring-forward 2:30 AM is handled per policy (skip/shift) and fall-back 2:30 AM ambiguity resolves to expected occurrence(s).

behaviors.description: When a scheduled job's computed next run time has already passed (e.g., system was down), the engine determines whether to skip or backfill based on the job's configured missed-run policy.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is orchestration/state handoff: computed next-run timestamp + policy config + decision path (skip vs backfill). This is cross-module engine behavior (MOCK-MISS=yes for module interaction). No real OS/network/subprocess required if clock/time inputs are controlled in-process, and no AI dependency. Rejected System because behavior text is policy decision logic, not infrastructure behavior under real process/network conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Wire calculator output into missed-run policy evaluator in an in-process engine test; simulate downtime by advancing current time past next-run and assert decision/output actions differ correctly for `skip` vs `backfill` policies.
